exercises.title: Minor GC vs Major GC — identify from GC log
exercises.instructions:
```
[2024-06-15T19:05:00] GC(42) Pause Young (Normal) (G1 Evacuation Pause) 12.3ms
[2024-06-15T19:05:30] GC(43) Pause Full (G1 Evacuation Pause) 832.1ms
[2024-06-15T19:06:00] GC(44) Pause Young (Normal) (G1 Evacuation Pause) 11.8ms
```

1. Which lines are Minor GC (Young Gen) and which is Major/Full GC?
2. Which one is causing the p99 latency spikes?
3. What is happening every 30 seconds?
exercises.expected_solution:
1. GC(42) and GC(44): Minor GC (Pause Young) — Young Generation collection
   GC(43): Major GC (Pause Full) — Full heap collection

2. GC(43) - Pause Full: 832.1ms is causing p99 latency spikes
   All application threads STOP for 832ms during Full GC
   Any request in-flight during that 832ms waits → shows as p99 spike

3. Every 30 seconds: Old Generation fills up (allocation rate > GC can reclaim)
   G1GC triggers Full GC to compact the Old Gen
   This repeats every ~30s because:
     - Heap may be undersized for the allocation rate
     - Too many long-lived objects promoted to Old Gen
     - MaxGCPauseMillis not tuned (default = 200ms, Full GC ignores this)
   Fix: -XX:MaxGCPauseMillis=100, increase heap, review object lifecycles

test_cases.expected:
1. GC(42) and GC(44): Minor GC (Pause Young) — Young Generation collection
   GC(43): Major GC (Pause Full) — Full heap collection

2. GC(43) - Pause Full: 832.1ms is causing p99 latency spikes
   All application threads STOP for 832ms during Full GC
   Any request in-flight during that 832ms waits → shows as p99 spike

3. Every 30 seconds: Old Generation fills up (allocation rate > GC can reclaim)
   G1GC triggers Full GC to compact the Old Gen
   This repeats every ~30s because:
     - Heap may be undersized for the allocation rate
     - Too many long-lived objects promoted to Old Gen
     - MaxGCPauseMillis not tuned (default = 200ms, Full GC ignores this)
   Fix: -XX:MaxGCPauseMillis=100, increase heap, review object lifecycles

hints: Pause Young = Minor GC (Young Gen only)
Pause Full = Major GC (all threads stop)
832ms every 30s = periodic Old Gen exhaustion

exercises.title: Complete the production JVM flags
exercises.instructions: Fill in the JVM flags for a production payment service.
hints: G1GC: default in Java 11+, balanced pause/throughput
MaxGCPauseMillis=100: 100ms target pause
75% heap leaves 25% for non-heap overhead

exercises.title: Read GC metrics from Spring Boot Actuator
exercises.instructions:
Show how to:
1. Enable GC logging in application.yml
2. Check jvm.gc.pause metrics via actuator
3. Write a Grafana PromQL for GC pause p99
4. Alert rule: GC pause > 200ms
exercises.expected_solution:
# application.yml:
# JVM GC logging (goes to stdout in containers, captured by log aggregation):
# logging.level.org.springframework.boot.context.embedded: DEBUG
# Spring Boot Actuator exposes JVM metrics automatically:
# management.endpoints.web.exposure.include: health,metrics,prometheus
# No extra config needed for jvm.gc.* metrics!

# Enable GC logging in JAVA_TOOL_OPTIONS:
# -Xlog:gc*:stdout:time,uptime,level,tags
# (stdout in containers → captured by your log system)

# GET /actuator/metrics/jvm.gc.pause → shows count, sum, max, percentiles
# Example response:
# { name: 'jvm.gc.pause',
#   measurements: [
#     { statistic: 'COUNT', value: 142 },
#     { statistic: 'MAX', value: 0.105 },  ← max GC pause = 105ms
#     { statistic: 'TOTAL_TIME', value: 1.42 }
#   ],
#   availableTags: [{tag: 'gc', values: ['G1 Young Generation', 'G1 Old Generation']}]
# }

# PROMETHEUS PromQL — P99 GC pause:
# histogram_quantile(0.99, sum(rate(jvm_gc_pause_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (le, gc))
# Shows: Young Gen GC p99 = 12ms, Old Gen GC p99 = 105ms

# GRAFANA ALERT:
# prometheus/rules.yml:
# - alert: JVMGCPauseHigh
#   expr: max(jvm_gc_pause_seconds_max) > 0.2
#   for: 2m
#   labels: { severity: warning }
#   annotations:
#     summary: 'JVM GC pause > 200ms for 2 minutes'
#     runbook_url: 'https://wiki.razorpay.com/runbooks/gc-pause-high'

public class GCMetricsDemo { public static void main(String[] a) {
    System.out.println("jvm.gc.pause: auto-exposed by Spring Boot Actuator + Micrometer");
    System.out.println("PromQL: histogram_quantile(0.99, rate(jvm_gc_pause_seconds_bucket[5m]))");
} }

expected_stdout_contains: jvm.gc.pause: auto-exposed by Spring Boot Actuator

hints: jvm.gc.* metrics: auto-registered by spring-boot-starter-actuator
jvm.gc.pause: Timer — includes count, sum, max, percentiles
Alert on jvm_gc_pause_seconds_max: catches worst-case GC pause

exercises.title: Configure JVM for low-latency with ZGC
exercises.instructions:
Configure ZGC for Zerodha's trading engine:
1. ZGC flags in JAVA_TOOL_OPTIONS
2. application.yml: GC metrics enabled
3. Compare: G1GC vs ZGC pause times
4. When NOT to use ZGC
exercises.expected_solution:
# ZGC configuration (Java 15+, recommended Java 17+):
# JAVA_TOOL_OPTIONS:
# -XX:+UseZGC
# -XX:SoftMaxHeapSize=1536m    # hint: try to stay under 1.5GB heap
# -XX:MaxRAMPercentage=75.0    # heap = 75% of container limit
# -XX:+UseContainerSupport
# -XX:+HeapDumpOnOutOfMemoryError
# -XX:HeapDumpPath=/tmp/
# No MaxGCPauseMillis needed — ZGC targets sub-ms by design

# application.yml: no extra config (Micrometer auto-detects ZGC)

# ZGC vs G1GC COMPARISON:
# G1GC:
#   Pause target: 100-200ms (achievable with tuning)
#   Pause actual: typically 10-100ms, occasionally 200-500ms
#   Throughput: higher than ZGC (less overhead)
#   Use for: most Spring Boot services
#
# ZGC:
#   Pause target: < 1ms (regardless of heap size!)
#   Pause actual: typically 0.1-1ms
#   Throughput: slightly lower (concurrent GC threads compete with app)
#   Memory: uses more memory overhead for barriers
#   Use for: latency-critical services (trading, payment, real-time bidding)
#   NOT recommended: high-throughput batch jobs (G1GC better)

# WHEN NOT TO USE ZGC:
# 1. Java < 15 (not available)
# 2. Batch processing (throughput more important than latency)
# 3. Very small heap (< 256MB): ZGC overhead not worth it
# 4. Limited CPU (ZGC needs CPU for concurrent collection)

public class ZGCConfig { public static void main(String[] a) {
    System.out.println("ZGC: sub-ms pauses, Java 17+, for latency-critical services"); } }
hints: ZGC: concurrent GC — no stop-the-world (except for very brief sync points)
SoftMaxHeapSize: hint, not hard limit (ZGC expands beyond if needed)
Throughput tradeoff: ZGC uses ~5-10% more CPU for concurrent collection

exercises.title: Detect and fix a ThreadLocal memory leak
exercises.instructions:
Simulate a ThreadLocal memory leak and show the fix:
1. A request filter stores data in ThreadLocal but never clears it
2. Thread pool reuse: ThreadLocal grows unbounded
3. Fix: always remove in finally block
4. @SpringBootTest: verify no memory growth after 100 requests
hints: ThreadLocal.remove(): ALWAYS call in finally block (like MDC.clear())
ThreadLocal.withInitial(): creates empty map on first access
Leak symptom: heap grows at rate of requests × per-request data size

exercises.title: Heap dump analysis workflow
exercises.instructions:
Document the heap dump analysis workflow:
1. Enable HeapDumpOnOutOfMemoryError in Dockerfile
2. How to get a heap dump from a running pod (jmap)
3. Open with VisualVM: find largest retained heap
4. Common culprits: static collections, ThreadLocal, unclosed streams
5. Fix pattern for each

expected_stdout_contains: Heap dump: jmap -dump → copy → analyze with MAT

hints: jmap: JVM tool for heap dumps from running JVM
MAT Leak Suspects: auto-identifies top candidates
Dominator Tree: object A dominates B if A is on every path from GC root to B

exercises.title: Monitor GC metrics with Micrometer @SpringBootTest
exercises.instructions:
Write a test that:
1. Gets the jvm.gc.pause Timer from MeterRegistry
2. Allocates objects to trigger Minor GC
3. Checks GC metrics are recorded
4. Shows: GC count increases after allocation
5. Alert threshold: max > 200ms
hints: jvm.gc.pause Timer: registered by Micrometer JvmGcMetrics auto-config
jvm.memory.used Gauge: always > 0 in a running JVM
registry.getMeters(): all registered meters — Spring Boot registers many automatically

exercises.title: Memory sizing for different service profiles
exercises.instructions:
Calculate JVM memory settings for 3 Razorpay services:
1. payment-service: 2GB container, high throughput, G1GC
2. fraud-detector: 4GB container, ML model loaded, ZGC
3. notification-worker: 512MB container, batch processing

For each: MaxRAMPercentage, expected heap, expected non-heap, GC flags.
hints: MaxRAMPercentage=70% for small containers: more of the 30% goes to Spring Boot's own Metaspace
ZGC shines with large heaps: pause time is O(1) regardless of heap size
Batch: throughput matters more → G1GC with larger Young Gen

exercises.title: Allocation rate tracking with Micrometer
exercises.instructions:
Write AllocationRateMonitor:
1. Track object allocation rate using Micrometer Gauge
2. Warn if allocation rate > 500MB/s (risk of GC pressure)
3. Use jvm.gc.memory.allocated counter (auto-provided)
4. @Scheduled: log rate every 60 seconds
hints: jvm.gc.memory.allocated: cumulative bytes allocated (monotonically increasing counter)
Allocation rate = delta_bytes / delta_seconds: rate over time window
> 500MB/s: typical GC pressure threshold for most services

exercises.title: What does this JVM flag do and why is it wrong?
exercises.instructions:
Review these JVM flags. For each: is it correct? If wrong, what's the problem?

A: `-Xmx4g -Xms1g` in a 4GB container
B: `-XX:MaxGCPauseMillis=1` for a payment service
C: `-XX:+DisableExplicitGC`
D: `-Xss256k` for a service with deep recursion
E: No HeapDumpOnOutOfMemoryError flag
exercises.expected_solution:
A: -Xmx4g -Xms1g in a 4GB container → WRONG
   4GB container × JVM non-heap = 4GB + metaspace + threads ≈ OOMKilled!
   JVM will try to allocate 4GB heap. Container has 4GB total.
   Non-heap (Metaspace, thread stacks, JIT code cache) adds ~512MB-1GB.
   Result: JVM tries to use 4.5-5GB → OOMKilled.
   Fix: -XX:MaxRAMPercentage=75.0 → heap = 3GB, leaves 1GB for non-heap.
   Also: Xms≠Xmx causes heap resize pauses. Use -XX:MaxRAMPercentage=75.0 only.

B: -XX:MaxGCPauseMillis=1 for payment service → PROBLEMATIC
   1ms pause target: extremely aggressive. G1GC will try to collect very small
   amounts per GC cycle to keep pauses < 1ms. This dramatically increases GC
   frequency (hundreds of GC cycles per second). Result: GC overhead so high
   that throughput suffers (application threads compete with GC threads).
   Fix: 100ms is a good target for payment services. Use ZGC if sub-ms is needed.

C: -XX:+DisableExplicitGC → USUALLY CORRECT
   Prevents System.gc() calls from triggering Full GC.
   Some libraries call System.gc() which causes avoidable pauses.
   Safe to add in most services. Exception: some off-heap memory cleanup
   relies on explicit GC (e.g., old versions of NIO direct buffers).

D: -Xss256k for deep recursion → WRONG
   Each thread stack = 256KB. Default: 512KB-1MB.
   Deep recursion needs more stack space → StackOverflowError.
   -Xss256k: saves memory but risky for recursive code (parsers, XML, JSON).
   Fix: use default (-Xss512k or system default) unless memory is very constrained.

E: No HeapDumpOnOutOfMemoryError → CRITICAL OMISSION
   When OOM occurs: JVM exits with no forensic evidence.
   Without heap dump: impossible to know which objects caused OOM.
   ALWAYS add: -XX:+HeapDumpOnOutOfMemoryError -XX:HeapDumpPath=/tmp/

test_cases.expected:
A: -Xmx4g -Xms1g in a 4GB container → WRONG
   4GB container × JVM non-heap = 4GB + metaspace + threads ≈ OOMKilled!
   JVM will try to allocate 4GB heap. Container has 4GB total.
   Non-heap (Metaspace, thread stacks, JIT code cache) adds ~512MB-1GB.
   Result: JVM tries to use 4.5-5GB → OOMKilled.
   Fix: -XX:MaxRAMPercentage=75.0 → heap = 3GB, leaves 1GB for non-heap.
   Also: Xms≠Xmx causes heap resize pauses. Use -XX:MaxRAMPercentage=75.0 only.

B: -XX:MaxGCPauseMillis=1 for payment service → PROBLEMATIC
   1ms pause target: extremely aggressive. G1GC will try to collect very small
   amounts per GC cycle to keep pauses < 1ms. This dramatically increases GC
   frequency (hundreds of GC cycles per second). Result: GC overhead so high
   that throughput suffers (application threads compete with GC threads).
   Fix: 100ms is a good target for payment services. Use ZGC if sub-ms is needed.

C: -XX:+DisableExplicitGC → USUALLY CORRECT
   Prevents System.gc() calls from triggering Full GC.
   Some libraries call System.gc() which causes avoidable pauses.
   Safe to add in most services. Exception: some off-heap memory cleanup
   relies on explicit GC (e.g., old versions of NIO direct buffers).

D: -Xss256k for deep recursion → WRONG
   Each thread stack = 256KB. Default: 512KB-1MB.
   Deep recursion needs more stack space → StackOverflowError.
   -Xss256k: saves memory but risky for recursive code (parsers, XML, JSON).
   Fix: use default (-Xss512k or system default) unless memory is very constrained.

E: No HeapDumpOnOutOfMemoryError → CRITICAL OMISSION
   When OOM occurs: JVM exits with no forensic evidence.
   Without heap dump: impossible to know which objects caused OOM.
   ALWAYS add: -XX:+HeapDumpOnOutOfMemoryError -XX:HeapDumpPath=/tmp/

hints: MaxRAMPercentage: better than -Xmx in containers (scales with container limit)
MaxGCPauseMillis=1: too aggressive → GC runs too often → throughput suffers
HeapDumpOnOutOfMemoryError: non-negotiable production requirement

exercises.title: Complete JVM observability: GC + memory + threads
exercises.instructions:
Build JVMObservabilityService:
1. Gauge: current heap used vs max
2. Counter: track JVM warning events (heap > 80%)
3. @Scheduled: check heap every 30s, alert if > 80%
4. @SpringBootTest (2 tests): gauges registered, alert threshold correct
hints: MemoryMXBean: JVM management API for heap/non-heap usage
getMax(): maximum heap (-Xmx or container-based); -1 if no limit set
80% threshold: common alert point before OOM becomes likely

exercises.title: GC tuning experiment: before and after comparison
exercises.instructions:
Write a load simulation that:
1. Creates lots of short-lived objects (simulates payment processing)
2. Measure GC pause before tuning (default flags)
3. After tuning: -XX:MaxGCPauseMillis=100, increased Young Gen
4. Show: expected improvement in GC metrics
5. @SpringBootTest: GC pause timer accessible
hints: jvm.gc.pause may be empty before first GC — test infrastructure, not specific pause values
jvm.memory.used{area=heap}: always available, current heap usage
GC tuning experiment: production load test, not unit test

exercises.title: Identify memory leak from simulated heap growth
exercises.instructions:
Write a test that:
1. Simulates a memory leak (static collection growing)
2. Detects it via heap usage increase over time
3. Shows the fix
4. @SpringBootTest: verify fixed version doesn't grow
5. Use ManagementFactory.getMemoryMXBean()
hints: Caffeine maximumSize: evicts LRU when size exceeded
expireAfterWrite: time-based eviction regardless of size
MemoryMXBean.getHeapMemoryUsage(): current JVM heap state

exercises.title: JVM warm-up and JIT compilation tracking
exercises.instructions:
Spring Boot services are slow for the first few seconds after startup — JIT hasn't compiled hot paths yet.
1. Explain: JIT tiers (C1 → C2)
2. Track JIT compilation time via JMX
3. Warm-up strategy: make requests to key endpoints on startup
4. @SpringBootTest: JIT metrics accessible
5. Production pattern: k8s readiness delay for JIT warm-up
hints: CompilationMXBean: tracks total JIT compilation time
isCompilationTimeMonitoringSupported(): not all JVMs support this
k8s readinessProbe initialDelaySeconds: accounts for JIT warm-up time

exercises.title: Complete JVM performance observability — M22 T1 synthesis
exercises.instructions:
Build complete Razorpay JVM observability:
1. Heap utilization gauge (with 80% alert)
2. GC pause monitoring (from jvm.gc.pause)
3. Memory leak simulation and fix (bounded Caffeine cache)
4. JIT compilation time tracking
5. @SpringBootTest (3 tests): heap gauge, memory bounded, JIT trackable

All 3 tests pass.
exercises.starter_code: // Complete M22 T1 JVM observability synthesis
hints: Caffeine maximumSize: eviction happens asynchronously, allow margin (≤ 600)
heapUtilization: may be > 0.8 in test JVM — assert ≥ 0 && ≤ 1.0
jitTime: always ≥ 0 after Spring Boot starts (JIT is always running)
